result:
The art of lingering has become, in our accelerated century, something approaching a revolutionary act. I sit on a wooden bench in a park whose name I never bothered to learn, watching late-afternoon light filter through a canopy of oaks, and I wonder when exactly stillness became so difficult. The bench itself is weathered, its green paint peeling in curls like dried leaves, revealing grey wood beneath. A brass plaque embedded in the slats reads “In memory of Eleanor Vance, who loved this view.” I never met Eleanor, but I find myself grateful to her, or to whoever loved her enough to memorialize her in this spot. She has given me permission to pause, an inheritance of stillness passed down from a stranger.

We are all, I suspect, conducting a series of private negotiations with time. The twenty-first century markets itself as an era of unprecedented connectivity, yet what it connects us to, primarily, is a relentless present tense, a perpetual now that thins out our experience of duration. Notifications arrive not as interruptions but as rhythms, a systolic pulse of urgency that keeps us suspended in a state of low-grade emergency. Even now, sitting here, I feel the phantom vibration of a phone in my pocket that isn’t there—I left it at home deliberately, an experiment in amputation. The first hour was jittery, an almost physical withdrawal, my hand repeatedly reaching for the absent rectangle as an amputee might reach for a missing limb. But now, entering my second hour of disconnection, something is beginning to shift. The world is starting to thicken.

Consider the oak tree in front of me. I’ve walked past it dozens of times before, probably, without really seeing it. It’s a coast live oak, I think, its branches twisting out with that muscular irregularity that distinguishes it from the more orderly deciduous trees the city planners favor. Moss grows in the crevices of its bark, a miniature topography of green valleys running down the ridgelines of the trunk. One branch in particular draws my eye—it juts out almost horizontally, then elbows sharply upward, defying the logic of gravity, a wooden question mark hanging in the air. An acorn woodpecker has been drilling holes in this branch, neat rows of them, each one stuffed with an acorn, the tree transformed into a granary. I sit here and watch the bird working, a black-and-white flicker with a red cap, drilling with mechanical precision, inserting the acorn, tapping it into place, then flying off for another. I have never seen this before. I have walked past this tree, this bird, this intricate relationship between species, and remained utterly unaware. How much of the world have I missed simply because I was moving too quickly to notice?

This is not a new lament. The complaint that life is speeding up, that we are losing our capacity for deep attention, recurs throughout human history. The ancient Stoics warned against the distracted life; medieval monks cultivated practices of contemplation precisely because distraction was a recognized enemy of the soul. What is new, perhaps, is the sheer systematic nature of our distraction, the way it has been engineered into the very infrastructure of daily life. We carry in our pockets devices designed by teams of brilliant engineers whose express purpose is to capture and hold our attention for as long as possible, to monetize the minutes and hours of our lives. The result is a peculiar form of cognitive fragmentation. I'll be reading a book and find myself unable to follow a complex paragraph, my brain twitching toward the lure of a quick dopamine hit from social media. I'll sit down to write and discover that my mind has been colonized by a dozen petty anxieties, none of them urgent, all of them clamoring.

But I don’t want to be merely elegiac. There is something condescending in the way we talk about distraction, as if we are all helpless victims of forces beyond our control. The truth is more complicated. We participate in our own fragmentation. We choose the quick scroll over the long read not because we are forced to but because the quick scroll is easier, demands less of us, asks nothing in return. Deep attention is hard. It requires a willingness to be bored, to push through the initial resistance, to sit with the discomfort of a mind that has been trained to ping-pong from stimulus to stimulus. The woodpecker returns with another acorn, and I marvel at its focus. It is not, I realize, that the bird possesses some superhuman concentration; it is simply doing what its nature demands, without the internal commentary, the self-sabotaging voice that tells me I should be doing something else, something more productive, something that will look better on a list of accomplishments.

There’s a squirrel now, chattering at the woodpecker from a higher branch, its tail flicking with the outrage of the entitled. The woodpecker ignores it entirely, and after a moment the squirrel gives up and scampers down the trunk in that helical spiral squirrels use, disappearing into the undergrowth. This tiny drama played out over perhaps forty seconds, and I caught it only because I was sitting still long enough to see it. How many such dramas unfold around us every day, unseen? The park is a theatre, I realize, staging continuous performances for an audience that never shows up.

This thought branches off into another, as thoughts will do when given room to roam. I recall a line from the philosopher Simone Weil: “Attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity.” She wrote this in the context of prayer and the love of God, but it applies equally well to the secular world, perhaps even more so. To pay attention to something—truly, sustained, patient attention—is an act of love. It says: you are worth my time. And time, after all, is the one non-renewable resource, the one thing we can never get back. When I give my attention to a friend who is speaking, I am giving them a portion of my life. When I give my attention to the woodpecker, to the oak tree, to the shifting light, I am giving them my life, and in some mysterious way, that gift enlarges my life rather than depleting it.

I think about this often in the context of art-making. I am, by profession, a writer, which means I spend an enormous amount of time alone in a room staring at a screen or a piece of paper, trying to coax words into arrangements that might move someone else. The process is notoriously inefficient; a good day might yield a few hundred usable words, a bad day might yield nothing but the slow erosion of self-confidence. And yet, the only way to produce those few hundred words is to show up and pay attention—to the emerging shape of the idea, to the rhythm of the sentences, to the small, almost imperceptible cues that language offers if you are quiet enough to hear them. Writing, I’ve come to believe, is not fundamentally an act of expression but an act of attention. You pay attention to the world, and then you pay attention to the words that might capture some fragment of that world, and then you pay attention to the reader who will, if you’re lucky and reasonably skilled, pay attention to the words in turn. It is an entire economy of attention, a chain of generosity that links writer and reader across time and space.

This bench, this park, Eleanor Vance—she has become a link in that chain without intending it. Her name on the plaque prompted me to sit here, and sitting here prompted me to notice the oak and the woodpecker, and noticing those things prompted these sentences, which you, whoever you are, are now reading. Eleanor Vance, dead perhaps decades, has paid attention to you indirectly, through me. I find this idea enormously comforting. It suggests that attention is not wasted, even when it seems to have no immediate practical outcome. The attention you pay to the world ripples outward in ways you cannot predict and will never fully know.

The light is changing now, the sun dropping low enough that the shadows have lengthened into spindly, stretching versions of the trees that cast them. The gold of the afternoon is giving way to the amber of early evening, and a breeze has picked up, carrying the scent of freshly cut grass from somewhere deeper in the park. I realize I am hungry, and that I have been sitting here for what must be close to three hours. Three hours without a phone, without a task, without a purpose beyond simply being present. It should feel like wasted time, but it doesn’t. It feels like the most important thing I’ve done all week.

There is a paradox at the heart of contemporary life, which is that we have more time-saving devices than any civilization in history and yet we feel perpetually short of time. The washing machine, the automobile, the computer, the smartphone—each of these was supposed to liberate us from drudgery and give us more leisure. Instead, they have mostly accelerated the pace of life, filling the liberated hours with new activities, new obligations, new forms of consumption. The speed of information transfer has increased exponentially, and we have responded by trying to process more information, as if that were the same as understanding more. It is not. Understanding requires slowness. Understanding requires the kind of lingering that our culture no longer values.

I read a study recently—ironically, online—that claimed the average person in the developed world touches their phone over two thousand times a day. Two thousand times. That’s more than a hundred times per waking hour, almost two interactions per minute. Even accounting for hyperbole and flawed methodology, the number is staggering. It suggests a population so anxious, so desperate for stimulation, so terrified of their own unmediated thoughts that they cannot go a single minute without reaching for a digital pacifier. I am not exempt from this critique; I am describing my own behavior as much as anyone’s. The difference is that I’m trying, fitfully and inconsistently, to notice it, to build small pockets of resistance, like this afternoon in the park.

The resistance takes many forms. Sometimes it’s a walk without earbuds, letting the sounds of the city—sirens, birdsong, overheard fragments of conversation—mingle with the noise in my own head. Sometimes it’s cooking a meal from scratch, paying attention to the transformation of raw ingredients into something nourishing, the alchemy of heat and time. Sometimes it’s reading a difficult book, the kind that demands to be read slowly, with frequent pauses for reflection. Sometimes it’s simply sitting on a bench, watching the light change, letting the mind wander where it will.

Mind-wandering, incidentally, has a bad reputation in our productivity-obsessed age. We call it daydreaming, woolgathering, spacing out. But neuroscientists are beginning to understand that the mind’s default mode—the state it enters when not focused on an external task—is crucial for creativity, self-reflection, and moral reasoning. It is in these unfocused moments that we make unexpected connections, resolve lingering problems, and construct the narrative of our own lives. The constant stimulation of the digital world, the endless scrolling and refreshing, short-circuits this process. We are, quite literally, preventing our brains from doing the work they need to do. We are starving our minds of the unstructured time that generates wisdom.

I say all this, and yet I am acutely aware of the hypocrisy. This essay itself will likely be read on a screen, perhaps skimmed while the reader is half-distracted by other tabs, other notifications, the same digital noise I’m decrying. The medium taints the message. But maybe that’s okay. Maybe the goal isn’t purity but intention. The woodpecker doesn’t reflect on the ethics of its granary-building; it just builds. I can’t retreat from the modern world, and I’m not sure I’d want to even if I could. What I can do is try to carve out spaces—temporal, spatial, psychological—where the logic of acceleration is suspended, if only for an afternoon.

The park is emptying now. A jogger passes, earbuds in, eyes fixed straight ahead, and I feel a pang of kinship even in our mutual isolation. She’s probably trying to outrun something too, some silent pressure, some drumbeat of anxiety that only subsides when the body is pushed to its limit. We all have our strategies for coping, our private rituals for reclaiming a sense of agency in a world that seems to accelerate beyond our control. Mine involves old benches and borrowed memories of women named Eleanor.

The sky is doing something extraordinary now—the sun has dropped below the horizon but its light still catches a bank of high clouds, turning them into streaks of pink and coral, a brushfire in the upper atmosphere. A planet appears, Venus probably, bright and steady in the deepening blue. I remember learning once that the starlight we see is always old light, light that traveled years or centuries or millennia to reach our eyes. Looking at the night sky is literally looking into the past. There’s a metaphor there about attention and time, I’m sure, but the moment feels too delicate to dissect. Some things are better left unparsed.

I stand up from the bench, and my body registers the long sitting with a satisfying stiffness. I walk over to the oak tree and place my hand on its bark. It’s rougher than I expected, deeply furrowed, and slightly warm, still holding the day’s heat. The woodpecker’s granary is above my head, hundreds of acorns wedged into their individual holes, a winter’s worth of labor stored in a living pantry. I think about the patience required for that work, the untold trips back and forth, the unerring focus. The bird doesn’t worry about the meaning of its life; it just lives it, fully, completely, each moment occupied by the task at hand.

I walk home slowly, choosing the longer route through the residential streets rather than the direct path along the main road. The houses are modest here, bungalows from the 1920s with wide porches and established gardens, and lamps are beginning to come on in the windows, warm rectangles of light that hint at the domestic scenes unfolding within. I am not in a hurry, which is a delicious feeling, almost decadent. The evening air is soft and carries the smell of jasmine, that heady Southern perfume that always surprises me with its intensity. I breathe it in and think: this is the world. Not the world of headlines or deadlines or digital metrics, but the actual, physical world, the world of scent and touch and fading light, the world that existed long before we invented ways to distract ourselves from it and will exist long after we’re gone.

It’s hard to hold onto this perspective. I know that within hours of returning home, I will plug back into the grid, check the messages that have accumulated in my absence, feel the familiar anxious tug of the unfinished tasks and the unanswered emails. The spell will break, and the afternoon in the park will recede into memory, a pleasant anomaly in an otherwise hurried week. But maybe that’s enough. Maybe the point isn’t to sustain the state of lingering indefinitely—that’s utopian, and probably undesirable—but to remind myself periodically that it’s possible. To build into the architecture of my days regular opportunities for disconnection, for stillness, for the kind of deep attention that feels, in my better moments, like a form of prayer.

There’s a Jewish practice called Shabbat, a day of rest, which involves, among other things, refraining from creative work, from the manipulation of the world. For twenty-five hours, you step out of the river of productivity and simply exist, receiving the world rather than imposing yourself upon it. I am not observant in any traditional sense, but the wisdom of this practice seems more urgent than ever. We need structures, rituals, disciplines of attention, because the default culture offers none. If we don’t build them deliberately, they won’t exist. The default culture will fill every available moment with stimulation and call it freedom.

By the time I reach my front door, the sky has deepened to indigo, and more stars are visible. I stand on the doorstep for a moment, looking up, and think about the scale of things. This galaxy, this planet, this life—it’s all so unimaginably vast and so unimaginably brief. To waste even a portion of it in a state of distracted semi-attention seems, in moments like this, like a kind of tragedy. Not a grand, operatic tragedy, but a quiet one, a slow erosion of the only thing we really have: our experience of being alive.

I unlock the door and step inside. The house is quiet. My phone is on the kitchen counter where I left it, its screen dark, not yet reawakened to its role as a portal to the endless scroll. I look at it for a moment, then turn away and boil water for tea. I’ll check it later. Or maybe I won’t. The messages will keep. The world will continue its frantic spin. But for now, there is jasmine tea and a window that faces west, still faintly luminous with the memory of the sun. For now, that’s enough.

Later, much later, I find myself thinking about Eleanor Vance again. I know nothing about her beyond that plaque, but I find myself constructing a life for her anyway. I imagine her as an elderly woman who walked to this park every evening for decades, who knew the oaks and the squirrels and the seasonal rhythms of the wildflowers. I imagine her sitting on this very bench, before it bore her name, watching the same play of light and shadow, offering the same gift of attention that I received today. Maybe she was a painter, or a poet, or simply a woman who understood, without needing to articulate it, the value of lingering. And when she died, someone remembered that she loved this view, and they placed a bench here so that others might sit and share it.

It’s a small immortality, but perhaps the only kind that matters. Not fame, not legacy, but this: the quiet transmission of attention from one soul to another, across the boundary of death. You sit on my bench, Eleanor says, across the years, and I sit, and I write this, and you read it, and the chain continues. We are all, I think, custodians of an attention that is larger than any one of us. We can squander it, or we can pass it on. The woodpecker drills its holes, the acorns are stored against the winter, the planet swings through its orbit, and somewhere in a park whose name I never learned, a bench waits for the next sitter, the next pause, the next small rebellion against the tyranny of speed.

I unlock the door and step inside. The house is quiet. My phone is on the kitchen counter where I left it, its screen dark, not yet reawakened to its role as a portal to the endless scroll. I look at it for a moment, then turn away and boil water for tea. I’ll check it later. Or maybe I won’t. The messages will keep. The world will continue its frantic spin. But for now, there is jasmine tea and a window that faces west, still faintly luminous with the memory of the sun. For now, that’s enough.

Later, much later, I find myself thinking about Eleanor Vance again. I know nothing about her beyond that plaque, but I find myself constructing a life for her anyway. I imagine her as an elderly woman who walked to this park every evening for decades, who knew the oaks and the squirrels and the seasonal rhythms of the wildflowers. I imagine her sitting on this very bench, before it bore her name, watching the same play of light and shadow, offering the same gift of attention that I received today. Maybe she was a painter, or a poet, or simply a woman who understood, without needing to articulate it, the value of lingering. And when she died, someone remembered that she loved this view, and they placed a bench here so that others might sit and share it.

It’s a small immortality, but perhaps the only kind that matters. Not fame, not legacy, but this: the quiet transmission of attention from one soul to another, across the boundary of death. You sit on my bench, Eleanor says, across the years, and I sit, and I write this, and you read it, and the chain continues. We are all, I think, custodians of an attention that is larger than any one of us. We can squander it, or we can pass it on. The woodpecker drills its holes, the acorns are stored against the winter, the planet swings through its orbit, and somewhere in a park whose name I never learned, a bench waits for the next sitter, the next pause, the next small rebellion against the tyranny of speed.

I'll craft a thoughtful, flowing narrative.
